title: Klinefelter Syndrome (47,XXY): Lifelong Guidelines-Based Follow-Up and TRT Monitoring Protocol
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: hypergonadotropic hypogonadism and post-pubertal gynecomastia on testosterone undecanoate (Inj Cernos 1000 mg); establishing the replacement schedule; CBC/haematocrit and LFT surveillance; and the standing multisystem monitoring calendar KS demands from diagnosis to adulthood
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case (Endocrine Society / EAA–ISSAM guideline framework)

relatedChapters.title: Klinefelter Syndrome in Adults — Trough Symptoms & Surveillance

patient.chiefComplaint: Established Klinefelter syndrome on testosterone replacement; bilateral post-pubertal gynecomastia persisting despite treatment.
pmh: Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (low testosterone, elevated LH and FSH)
Bilateral post-pubertal gynecomastia (glandular, present since adolescence)
Small firm testes (<4 mL bilaterally), azoospermia
On testosterone undecanoate (Inj Cernos 1000 mg IM) — establishing/maintaining replacement schedule
medications: Testosterone undecanoate (Cernos) 1000 mg IM — maintenance regimen under endocrine supervision

caseDescription: A man with confirmed Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) presents for structured long-term care. He has hypergonadotropic hypogonadism and bilateral post-pubertal gynecomastia that did not regress after puberty. The core treatment issue is establishing and maintaining an effective exogenous testosterone replacement schedule with Inj Cernos 1000 mg (testosterone undecanoate), while executing the guideline-mandated safety monitoring — especially full blood count/haematocrit (CBC) and liver function tests (LFTs) — and the broader lifelong multisystem surveillance protocol KS requires (bone, metabolic, breast cancer, fertility, psychosocial, autoimmune). This module teaches the standing follow-up calendar, not a one-off visit.
keyFindings: 47,XXY — primary (hypergonadotropic) hypogonadism: low T, high LH/FSH
Post-pubertal gynecomastia — fibrotic glandular tissue unlikely to regress fully on TRT alone
Cernos 1000 mg (testosterone undecanoate) — long-acting IM depot; approved interval 10–14 weeks
CBC/haematocrit is the critical TRT safety monitor (polycythaemia risk)
LFTs monitor for NAFLD (common in KS) and injection-related effects
Lifelong structured surveillance — not ad hoc follow-up
finalDiagnosis: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism and persistent post-pubertal gynecomastia, on testosterone undecanoate (Cernos 1000 mg). Plan: maintain TRT with trough testosterone-guided interval individualisation (10–14 weeks); monitor CBC/haematocrit every 3 months initially then 6-monthly, LFTs 6-monthly, plus the full lifelong surveillance calendar (DXA, metabolic screen, annual breast exam, psychosocial screening, thyroid/autoimmune). Address gynecomastia: TRT optimisation first; consider surgical subcutaneous mastectomy for persistent fibrotic tissue; never dismiss a new breast mass (20–50× breast cancer risk).
learningObjectives: Interpret the hypergonadotropic hypogonadism pattern in 47,XXY and initiate testosterone undecanoate correctly.
Execute the CBC/haematocrit and LFT monitoring protocol on TRT with action thresholds.
Manage post-pubertal gynecomastia in KS — TRT, medical options, surgery, and cancer vigilance.
Deliver the lifelong, guidelines-based multisystem surveillance schedule from diagnosis onward.
Counsel on fertility, psychosocial needs, and the dangers of unsupervised self-adjustment.

items.title: How to frame lifelong KS care — not a prescription refill, but a standing protocol
items.content: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) is a lifelong multisystem disorder, not a testosterone-deficiency label that ends at the first injection. This patient has hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (low testosterone with elevated LH and FSH confirming primary testicular failure) and bilateral post-pubertal gynecomastia — glandular breast tissue that established during adolescence and persists into adulthood. The immediate treatment task is establishing and maintaining an effective testosterone undecanoate schedule (Inj Cernos 1000 mg IM) with mandatory safety monitoring of CBC/haematocrit and LFTs. The broader task is implementing the guidelines-based lifelong follow-up protocol covering bone, metabolic, oncological, fertility, psychosocial and autoimmune domains. Every visit should advance both TRT optimisation and surveillance — never one without the other.
keyPoints: KS = lifelong multisystem disease, not just 'low testosterone'
Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism + post-pubertal gynecomastia in this case
TRT establishment + CBC/LFT safety monitoring + standing surveillance protocol

items.title: Why 47,XXY produces hypergonadotropic hypogonadism — and why the gonadotropin pattern matters
items.content: The extra X chromosome drives progressive testicular dysgenesis: Leydig-cell dysfunction lowers testosterone, and Sertoli/germ-cell failure causes azoospermia in >95%. Because the defect is in the testis, negative feedback on the hypothalamic–pituitary axis is lost — LH and FSH rise (hypergonadotropic pattern). This distinguishes KS from central hypogonadism (low T with low/inappropriately normal gonadotropins). FSH is typically disproportionately elevated (seminiferous tubule damage). Monitoring LH/FSH at baseline and periodically confirms the primary pattern persists and helps detect superimposed pituitary disease if LH fails to rise appropriately — a trap described in cr-048 (Klinefelter plus macroadenoma).
keyPoints: 47,XXY → Leydig failure (↓T) + Sertoli/germ failure (azoospermia)
Primary pattern: low T + HIGH LH/FSH — opposite of central hypogonadism
Inappropriately normal LH with very low T → consider superimposed pituitary lesion (MRI)

items.title: Why post-pubertal gynecomastia in KS often persists despite testosterone replacement
items.content: Gynecomastia affects 50–70% of Klinefelter men, driven by a raised estradiol:testosterone ratio — low testosterone from Leydig failure plus aromatisation of adrenal androgens in adipose tissue. Pubertal gynecomastia that persists into adulthood becomes fibrotic: the glandular tissue undergoes stromal fibrosis and will not fully regress even when testosterone is optimised. Starting TRT may prevent worsening but rarely reverses established post-pubertal gynecomastia. Management options: (1) optimise testosterone first (adequate trough levels); (2) lifestyle measures to reduce adiposity and aromatisation; (3) low-dose tamoxifen or anastrozole for symptomatic persistent tissue (cautiously — bone effects of over-suppressed estradiol); (4) subcutaneous mastectomy for cosmetically significant fibrotic gynecomastia after TRT is stable. Critically: any new, unilateral, or hard mass is not 'just gynecomastia' — image and biopsy (20–50× breast cancer risk).
keyPoints: ↑E2:↓T ratio drives gynecomastia in 50–70% of KS men
Post-pubertal tissue fibroses — TRT alone rarely reverses it
Options: optimise TRT → lifestyle → tamoxifen/AI → surgery; new mass → image/biopsy

items.title: How to manage bilateral post-pubertal gynecomastia on Cernos — the decision sequence
items.content: Do not rush to surgery before TRT is established and stable. Step 1: confirm adequate testosterone replacement — draw a trough total testosterone on injection day (target mid-normal range, typically 400–700 ng/dL; adjust interval within 10–14 weeks if trough is low). Step 2: assess the tissue — true glandular gynecomastia (firm, concentric, retroareolar) vs lipomastia (soft, diffuse fat); document Tanner stage and whether tissue is progressive. Step 3: if TRT-optimised and tissue is stable, fibrotic, and causing psychological distress or pain, refer for subcutaneous mastectomy (liposuction alone is insufficient for glandular tissue). Step 4: annual clinical breast examination regardless — teach self-examination. Step 5: if fertility is desired, address before any surgical or hormonal manipulation that might affect reproductive planning.
keyPoints: Establish stable TRT with adequate trough before considering gynecomastia surgery
Subcutaneous mastectomy for fibrotic glandular tissue; liposuction alone fails
Annual breast exam + self-exam; fertility planning before surgery

items.title: How to establish Inj Cernos 1000 mg (testosterone undecanoate) — loading and maintenance
items.content: Testosterone undecanoate 1000 mg (Cernos) is a long-acting intramuscular depot. Standard initiation per product labelling: 1000 mg IM at baseline, repeat 1000 mg at 6 weeks (loading), then maintenance every 10–14 weeks (commonly 12-weekly initially, individualised by trough level). Inject deeply into the gluteal muscle; rotate sites. Before the first injection: baseline total and free testosterone, LH/FSH, estradiol, SHBG; full blood count with haematocrit; LFTs; PSA with digital rectal examination (age-appropriate); fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipids; vitamin D; DXA scan; and document karyotype. Counsel on expected effects (libido, energy, body composition over 3–6 months), injection-site reactions, and the mandatory monitoring schedule. Fertility must be addressed before starting if paternity is desired — TRT suppresses residual spermatogenesis.
keyPoints: Loading: 1000 mg at 0 and 6 weeks; maintenance every 10–14 weeks (often 12-weekly)
Baseline labs before first injection: hormones, CBC/Hct, LFTs, PSA/DRE, metabolic, DXA
Address fertility before TRT if paternity desired

items.title: Why trough testosterone — not symptoms alone — governs the Cernos interval
items.content: Testosterone undecanoate produces a peak at 2–4 weeks post-injection, a plateau at weeks 4–8, and a trough by weeks 10–14. The approved maintenance interval is 10–14 weeks, but individual clearance varies. At each monitoring visit, draw a trough total testosterone (and free testosterone if SHBG is abnormal) on the day of injection, before the dose. Target: trough in the mid-normal adult range (commonly 400–700 ng/dL). If trough <300 ng/dL with hypogonadal symptoms, shorten the interval (e.g. from 12 to 10 weeks). If trough is adequate but symptoms persist, search for other causes (anaemia, thyroid, depression, B12/vitamin D deficiency). Never adjust the interval on symptoms alone — anchor to trough level and haematocrit safety (see cr-028 for the trough-symptom debate).
keyPoints: Peak 2–4 wk, trough 10–14 wk — individualise interval by trough level
Trough T on injection day before dose; target mid-normal range
Low trough → shorten interval; adequate trough + symptoms → seek other cause

items.title: Why CBC and haematocrit are the mandatory TRT safety monitors — schedule and action thresholds
items.content: Testosterone stimulates erythropoiesis via erythropoietin and direct marrow effects — the most important acute safety concern on TRT is polycythaemia. Monitor full blood count with haematocrit: every 3 months during the first year of TRT, then every 6 months once stable; more frequently if the injection interval is shortened or haematocrit is borderline. Action thresholds (Endocrine Society / EAA framework): haematocrit ≤50% — continue current regimen; 52–54% — reduce dose or extend interval, recheck in 3 months; >54% — hold testosterone, therapeutic phlebotomy, resume at lower dose/longer interval once Hct <50%. Also watch for TRT-unrelated anaemia (B12, iron, chronic disease) that mimics TRT fatigue. CBC is non-negotiable at every TRT monitoring visit — not optional.
keyPoints: TRT → erythropoiesis → polycythaemia risk (VTE, stroke, MI)
CBC/Hct: 3-monthly year 1, then 6-monthly; more often if interval shortened
Hct >54% → hold TRT + phlebotomy; 52–54% → reduce/extend interval

items.title: Why LFTs are monitored on TRT in KS — NAFLD, injections, and action thresholds
items.content: Liver function tests (ALT, AST, GGT, bilirubin, albumin) are monitored every 6 months on TRT for two reasons in KS. First, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD/MASLD) is common in Klinefelter men — partly independent of obesity and testosterone level — and TRT may improve hepatic fat but does not eliminate the risk. Rising transaminases warrant abdominal ultrasound and metabolic assessment. Second, intramuscular testosterone undecanoate is generally hepatically safe (not 17-alkylated), but baseline and periodic LFTs establish the patient's norm and detect unrelated liver disease. Action: ALT/AST >3× ULN — investigate (ultrasound, alcohol, medications, viral hepatitis, iron studies); mild elevation (1–3× ULN) with NAFLD — lifestyle, weight management, repeat in 3 months; normal LFTs — continue monitoring 6-monthly.
keyPoints: NAFLD common in KS — partly independent of TRT; monitor LFTs 6-monthly
TU IM is not hepatotoxic (not 17-alkylated) but LFTs establish baseline
ALT >3× ULN → investigate; mild NAFLD → lifestyle + repeat

items.title: How to structure the year-1 TRT monitoring calendar on Cernos 1000 mg
items.content: Year 1 (establishment phase): Month 0 — baseline full panel (see initiation note). Week 6 — second loading injection + review. Month 3 — trough testosterone, CBC/Hct, LFTs, symptom review, injection technique check. Month 6 — trough T, CBC/Hct, LFTs, estradiol (if gynecomastia), PSA/DRE, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, vitamin D. Month 9 — CBC/Hct (if interval adjustment contemplated). Month 12 — full annual review: trough T, CBC/Hct, LFTs, PSA, metabolic panel, lipids, DXA if not done at baseline, clinical breast exam, PHQ-9/GAD-7, TSH/thyroid antibodies. Adjust Cernos interval at months 3, 6, or 12 based on trough testosterone and haematocrit. Document every change with rationale.
keyPoints: Year 1: baseline → load at 6 wk → monitor at 3, 6, 9, 12 months
Each visit: trough T + CBC/Hct minimum; LFTs at 6 and 12 months
Full annual review at 12 months includes all surveillance domains

items.title: How to structure lifelong follow-up after year 1 — the standing surveillance schedule
items.content: Once TRT is stable (adequate trough, safe haematocrit, patient adherent), transition to the lifelong schedule. Every 6 months: trough testosterone (on injection day), CBC/Hct, LFTs, symptom and adherence review. Annually: PSA with DRE (age-appropriate), fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipid profile, blood pressure, waist/BMI, clinical breast examination, PHQ-9/GAD-7 depression/anxiety screening, TSH and thyroid antibodies, vitamin D. Every 2 years: DXA scan (more often if osteopenic). At every visit: fertility counselling if relevant, medication review, injection-site check, patient education reinforcement. Multidisciplinary team: endocrinology (lead), primary care, andrology/reproductive medicine, psychology, with rheumatology/oncology referral as needed. Provide a written patient-held monitoring card with dates and targets.
keyPoints: 6-monthly: trough T, CBC/Hct, LFTs
Annual: PSA, metabolic, lipids, breast exam, psychosocial, thyroid
DXA every 2 years; written patient monitoring card; MDT care

items.title: Why DXA is mandatory in KS and how often to repeat
items.content: Hypogonadism — especially if testosterone replacement was delayed, inadequate, or interrupted — causes low bone mineral density and increased fracture risk in KS. TRT improves but may not fully normalise BMD if replacement started late. DXA (lumbar spine, hip, distal radius) at baseline before or at TRT initiation, repeated every 2 years (annually if osteopenic). Protect bone with adequate TRT, calcium 1000–1200 mg/day, vitamin D repletion (target >30 ng/mL), weight-bearing exercise, and smoking/alcohol avoidance. Treat T-score ≤−2.5 or fragility fracture with bisphosphonate or denosumab. Do not over-suppress estradiol with aromatase inhibitors — estradiol is important for male bone health.
keyPoints: Delayed/inadequate TRT → low BMD; DXA at baseline then every 2 years
Optimise TRT + calcium + vitamin D >30 + exercise
Bisphosphonate if T-score ≤−2.5; don't over-suppress E2

items.title: Why metabolic screening is annual even on adequate TRT
items.content: Klinefelter men have increased metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, and NAFLD risk that persists partly independently of testosterone level and obesity — adequate TRT improves body composition but does not abolish metabolic risk. Cardiovascular disease is a leading cause of death. Annual monitoring: waist circumference, BMI, fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile (LDL, HDL, triglycerides), blood pressure. Intervene with lifestyle (diet, aerobic and resistance exercise), metformin for prediabetes/metabolic syndrome, statins per cardiovascular risk guidelines, and antihypertensives as indicated. NAFLD: LFTs 6-monthly, ultrasound if transaminases elevated.
keyPoints: Metabolic risk partly independent of TRT — screen annually
CVD = leading cause of death in KS
Annual glucose/HbA1c/lipids/BP/waist; lifestyle + metformin/statin as indicated

items.title: Why breast cancer vigilance is a defined surveillance domain in KS
items.content: Male breast cancer risk is 20–50× higher in KS than 46,XY men — driven by the oestrogen:androgen imbalance, gynecomastia, and obesity. Absolute risk remains low but relative risk demands vigilance. Annual clinical breast examination by the clinician; teach breast self-examination monthly. Image (mammography/ultrasound) and biopsy any discrete mass, skin change, nipple retraction, or bloody discharge — never assume 'just gynecomastia.' Also screen for mediastinal germ-cell tumours (excess in 20s–30s): investigate unexplained cough, chest pain, or weight loss with chest imaging. Document breast exam findings at every annual review.
keyPoints: Breast cancer RR 20–50× in KS — annual clinical exam + taught self-exam
Image/biopsy any suspicious mass — not 'just gynecomastia'
Mediastinal germ-cell tumour excess in 20s–30s

items.title: How fertility counselling fits into every KS follow-up visit
items.content: >95% of KS men are azoospermic, but micro-TESE retrieves sperm in 40–70% and ICSI achieves live births. TRT suppresses any residual spermatogenesis — fertility must be addressed before or by interrupting TRT, not after years on Cernos. At every visit: ask about paternity desires; if yes and on TRT, refer to reproductive medicine for micro-TESE planning (trial off TRT with hCG ± FSH stimulation, or micro-TESE with cryopreservation). If no current desire, document counselling and explain TRT is not contraception. Options: assisted reproduction, donor sperm, adoption, child-free — without bias.
keyPoints: Micro-TESE 40–70% success; ICSI enables live births
TRT suppresses spermatogenesis — address fertility before/during TRT planning
TRT is not contraception; counsel every visit

items.title: Why PHQ-9 and GAD-7 belong in the annual surveillance protocol
items.content: KS carries high rates of depression, anxiety, body-image distress (gynecomastia, small testes, tall habitus), and grief around infertility — compounded by language/learning difficulties in many. Screen PHQ-9 (depression) and GAD-7 (anxiety) annually; refer to psychology/psychiatry when positive. Psychosocial wellbeing drives adherence to TRT and surveillance — a patient who disengages misses CBC monitoring and silently develops polycythaemia or osteoporosis. Signpost KS support groups; involve partners/family; offer psychosexual counselling. This is not optional 'soft' care — it is a surveillance domain with measurable outcomes.
keyPoints: High depression/anxiety + body-image/infertility grief in KS
PHQ-9/GAD-7 annually; refer when positive
Psychosocial wellbeing drives TRT adherence and monitoring engagement

items.title: Why thyroid and autoimmune screening is part of the annual calendar
items.content: The extra X chromosome predisposes KS men to autoimmune disease — Hashimoto's thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes, coeliac disease, SLE, RA, Sjögren's. Annual TSH and thyroid peroxidase antibodies; baseline coeliac serology (tissue transglutaminase IgA); further autoimmune testing guided by symptoms (ANA, RF, anti-CCP) rather than indiscriminate panels. Untreated hypothyroidism or coeliac disease causes fatigue mimicking TRT inadequacy — do not attribute all symptoms to testosterone alone.
keyPoints: Extra X → autoimmunity: thyroiditis, T1DM, coeliac, SLE, RA
Annual TSH + TPO antibodies; baseline coeliac screen
Fatigue may be autoimmune — not always low testosterone

items.title: Klinefelter syndrome — lifelong monitoring protocol checklist (guidelines-based)
items.content: TRT (Cernos 1000 mg): ☐ Loading 0 + 6 wk, then 10–14 wk maintenance. ☐ Trough T on injection day — 3-monthly year 1, 6-monthly stable. ☐ CBC/Hct — 3-monthly year 1, 6-monthly stable (Hct >54% → hold + phlebotomy). ☐ LFTs — 6-monthly. ☐ PSA/DRE — annually (age-appropriate). Bone: ☐ DXA baseline, every 2 years. Metabolic: ☐ Glucose/HbA1c, lipids, BP, waist — annually. Breast: ☐ Clinical exam annually + self-exam taught. Psychosocial: ☐ PHQ-9/GAD-7 annually. Thyroid/autoimmune: ☐ TSH/TPO annually; coeliac baseline. Fertility: ☐ Counsel every visit. Gynecomastia: ☐ Optimise TRT first; surgery if fibrotic/persistent; image new masses.
keyPoints: TRT: trough T + CBC/Hct + LFTs on standing schedule
Annual: metabolic, breast, psychosocial, thyroid
DXA every 2 years; fertility counsel every visit

items.question: Why is haematocrit monitored more frequently than liver function tests on testosterone undecanoate?
items.answer: Because polycythaemia is the most common and immediately dangerous acute complication of testosterone therapy — testosterone stimulates erythropoiesis, and an unchecked rise in haematocrit predisposes to venous thromboembolism, stroke, and myocardial infarction. This can develop within weeks to months of starting or intensifying TRT. LFT abnormalities from NAFLD evolve more slowly and are managed on a 6-monthly cycle. Haematocrit therefore requires tighter surveillance (3-monthly in year 1, 6-monthly thereafter) with defined action thresholds, whereas LFTs are important but less acutely life-threatening.
keyPoints: Polycythaemia = most acute TRT danger (VTE, stroke, MI)
Hct can rise within weeks — needs 3–6-monthly monitoring
LFTs/NAFLD evolve slowly — 6-monthly sufficient

items.question: How should Inj Cernos 1000 mg be initiated and monitored in a newly diagnosed KS patient with post-pubertal gynecomastia?
items.answer: Before first injection: baseline total/free testosterone, LH/FSH, estradiol, CBC/Hct, LFTs, PSA/DRE, glucose/HbA1c/lipids, vitamin D, DXA, karyotype confirmation; address fertility if paternity desired. Inject 1000 mg IM at baseline and repeat at 6 weeks (loading). Maintenance every 10–14 weeks (commonly 12-weekly). Monitor: trough testosterone and CBC/Hct at 3 months; add LFTs at 6 months; full annual review at 12 months including breast exam, psychosocial screen, and metabolic panel. Adjust interval by trough level. For gynecomastia: optimise TRT first; consider subcutaneous mastectomy if fibrotic tissue persists and causes distress after 12 months of stable replacement.
keyPoints: Baseline panel → load 0 + 6 wk → maintenance 10–14 wk
Trough T + CBC/Hct at 3 mo; LFTs at 6 mo; full review at 12 mo
Gynecomastia: optimise TRT 12 mo before surgery consideration

items.stem: A man with 47,XXY has testosterone 180 ng/dL, LH 22 mIU/mL, and FSH 38 mIU/mL. What is the correct classification?
options: Primary (hypergonadotropic) hypogonadism
Central (hypogonadotropic) hypogonadism
Normal gonadal function
Hyperandrogenic state
items.explanation: Low testosterone with elevated LH and FSH indicates primary testicular failure with lost negative feedback — hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, classic for Klinefelter syndrome. Central hypogonadism shows low/inappropriately normal gonadotropins.

items.stem: On Cernos 1000 mg, a patient's haematocrit rises to 55%. What is the correct action?
options: Hold testosterone, arrange therapeutic phlebotomy, resume at lower dose/longer interval once Hct <50%
Continue current regimen — mild polycythaemia is expected and benign
Switch to oral testosterone immediately
No action until haematocrit reaches 60%
items.explanation: Haematocrit >54% mandates holding TRT and therapeutic phlebotomy due to thrombotic risk. Resume at reduced dose or extended interval once safe. Continuing or waiting for higher levels is dangerous.

items.stem: A KS patient has bilateral post-pubertal gynecomastia persisting after 12 months of adequate TRT (trough T 520 ng/dL). What is the most appropriate next step for the gynecomastia?
options: Refer for subcutaneous mastectomy if tissue is fibrotic and causing significant distress
Increase Cernos dose to 2000 mg to shrink the breast tissue
Stop TRT until gynecomastia resolves spontaneously
Reassure — TRT will always reverse post-pubertal gynecomastia given time
items.explanation: Post-pubertal gynecomastia fibroses and rarely regresses on TRT alone after 12 months of adequate replacement. Subcutaneous mastectomy is appropriate for persistent fibrotic tissue causing distress. Increasing dose risks polycythaemia; stopping TRT is harmful; spontaneous regression is unlikely.

items.stem: What is the correct loading and maintenance schedule for testosterone undecanoate (Cernos) 1000 mg?
options: 1000 mg at baseline, 1000 mg at 6 weeks, then every 10–14 weeks
1000 mg once, then every 4 weeks indefinitely
500 mg weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly
1000 mg annually only
items.explanation: Standard TU protocol: 1000 mg IM at initiation, repeat at 6 weeks (loading), then maintenance every 10–14 weeks individualised by trough testosterone. Weekly or monthly dosing applies to short-acting esters, not TU depot.

items.stem: Why are LFTs monitored every 6 months in KS patients on testosterone undecanoate?
options: NAFLD is common in KS independent of TRT, and LFTs detect hepatic disease requiring intervention
Testosterone undecanoate IM is hepatotoxic like oral methyltestosterone
LFTs measure testosterone levels indirectly
LFT monitoring replaces the need for CBC
items.explanation: NAFLD/MASLD is prevalent in KS partly independently of TRT; 6-monthly LFTs screen for hepatic disease. TU IM is not hepatotoxic (not 17-alkylated). LFTs do not measure testosterone and do not replace CBC monitoring.

items.stem: During the first year on Cernos 1000 mg, how frequently should haematocrit be checked?
options: Every 3 months
Once at baseline only
Annually
Only if the patient develops headache or redness of face
items.explanation: Guidelines recommend CBC/haematocrit every 3 months during the first year of TRT when erythropoietic effects are being established, then every 6 months once stable. Baseline-only or symptom-triggered monitoring misses silent polycythaemia.

items.statement: Post-pubertal gynecomastia in Klinefelter syndrome typically regresses fully once testosterone replacement is optimised.
items.explanation: False. Post-pubertal gynecomastia undergoes stromal fibrosis and rarely regresses fully on TRT alone. Surgical subcutaneous mastectomy may be needed for persistent fibrotic tissue.

items.statement: Haematocrit should be checked on the day of testosterone injection, before the dose is given.
items.explanation: True. Checking at trough (injection day, before dose) captures the nadir and is the standard timing for both haematocrit and trough testosterone monitoring.

items.statement: Once TRT is stable, Klinefelter syndrome surveillance can be limited to testosterone injections without laboratory monitoring.
items.explanation: False. Lifelong structured surveillance continues indefinitely: CBC/Hct and LFTs 6-monthly, annual metabolic/breast/psychosocial/thyroid screens, DXA every 2 years. Injections alone miss polycythaemia, NAFLD, osteoporosis, and cancer risks.

items.assertion: CBC with haematocrit should be monitored more frequently than LFTs in the first year of testosterone undecanoate therapy.
items.reason: Testosterone stimulates erythropoiesis, and polycythaemia can develop acutely with thrombotic consequences.
items.explanation: Both true — polycythaemia is the acute TRT safety concern requiring 3-monthly Hct monitoring in year 1, while LFTs are monitored 6-monthly for slower-evolving NAFLD. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: Subcutaneous mastectomy should be offered immediately at KS diagnosis before starting testosterone.
items.reason: Post-pubertal gynecomastia tissue becomes fibrotic and rarely regresses on testosterone replacement alone.
items.explanation: Assertion false (optimise TRT first, allow 12 months stable replacement before surgery consideration) but reason true (fibrotic tissue rarely regresses on TRT). Hence option 3.

items.assertion: Testosterone replacement therapy is a reliable contraceptive in Klinefelter syndrome.
items.reason: TRT suppresses spermatogenesis but suppression is incomplete and variable in KS men.
items.explanation: Assertion false (TRT is not reliable contraception) but reason true (suppression is incomplete). Hence option 3.

items.question: List the investigations due at the 6-month visit in year 1 of Cernos 1000 mg therapy.
items.answer: Trough total testosterone (on injection day, before dose), full blood count with haematocrit, liver function tests (ALT, AST, GGT, bilirubin), estradiol if gynecomastia symptoms, PSA with digital rectal examination, fasting glucose and HbA1c, lipid profile, vitamin D, symptom and adherence review, and injection-site examination.

items.question: State the haematocrit action thresholds on testosterone therapy.
items.answer: ≤50% — continue current regimen. 52–54% — reduce dose or extend injection interval, recheck in 3 months. >54% — hold testosterone, arrange therapeutic phlebotomy, resume at lower dose or longer interval once haematocrit falls below ~50%.

items.question: Name five non-TRT surveillance domains in the lifelong KS follow-up protocol.
items.answer: (1) Bone — DXA every 2 years. (2) Metabolic/cardiovascular — annual glucose/HbA1c, lipids, blood pressure, waist. (3) Breast cancer — annual clinical exam and taught self-examination. (4) Psychosocial — annual PHQ-9/GAD-7. (5) Autoimmune/thyroid — annual TSH and thyroid antibodies, baseline coeliac screen. (Also acceptable: fertility counselling, mediastinal germ-cell vigilance.)
